spec_match.rationale: Cart-line streaming is the canonical motivating use case the chromestatus entry cites. Both columns POST to the real /register-trigger backend route and surface the accepted/dropped counts; the without-ctx column correctly shows drops past index 20.

interactive.rationale: Range slider for conversions-per-source + re-run button drive two parallel fetches and re-render two streams of report rows with per-conversion pills (reported, dropped: source cap).

blurb_alignment.rationale: Card promises a side-by-side backend-backed comparison of with/without trigger_context_id; page delivers exactly that with N up to 60 conversion rows visible.

edge_cases.rationale: Conversions = 1 case works (1 report, none dropped); doesn't surface the upper bound of trigger_context_id format (string, ASCII, max 64 chars), and doesn't show what happens when the backend returns an error (no try/catch around registerTrigger, so a server 500 throws and the run() promise rejects silently).

references.rationale: see-also links chromestatus.com/feature/5079048977645568 and the wicg spec section #trigger-context-id directly.

design_system.rationale: Variables throughout, pill.warn / pill.drop use --accent-amber / --accent-rose backgrounds with explicit --bg-ivory text so contrast is fine; report-row uses bg-stone + charcoal text.

openQuestions.title: Catch the backend rejection path
openQuestions.detail: registerTrigger() awaits fetch + .json() with no try/catch. If the backend route 500s or the JSON is malformed the unhandled rejection breaks the run() and leaves the summary stuck on the previous value. Wrap each fetch in try/catch and degrade to synthetic counts with a banner ('backend unreachable — using simulated cap of 20').

openQuestions.title: Render the actual header the backend emits
openQuestions.detail: The mocked Attribution-Reporting-Register-Trigger code listing in each column is static; the backend response includes a responsePreview field that's not surfaced here. Wire that into the column footer so the demo proves the server-side header generation matches the JSON the page sent.

openQuestions.title: Probe state misleading for AR Feature Policy
openQuestions.detail: The probe checks ('attributionReporting' in document.featurePolicy) but the Document Policy API isn't necessarily where attributionReporting lives — featurePolicy is deprecated and the right check is document.featurePolicy?.features?.()?.includes('attribution-reporting') or simply navigator.attributionReporting?.registerTrigger. The current probe will say 'available' even on browsers that don't support the API.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the trigger_context_id length cap
openQuestions.detail: Spec restricts trigger_context_id to a fixed character set / max length (currently 64 ASCII). Add an input next to the slider that exposes the field's length and rejects oversized values so the constraint is visible.

summary: Best of the four siblings: the side-by-side stream + per-row pills make the cap viscerally obvious, and the backend route is wired up. The weak spot is error handling — the probe lies on unsupported browsers, the fetch has no catch, and the spec's trigger_context_id constraints are invisible.
